data.title: Why Adobe Is A 'Strong Buy' Despite The AI Boogeyman
data.description:
Adobe (ADBE) is still hanging in there even if the stock price might imply otherwise. AI continues to improve rapidly, increasing investor concern for the future outlook. ADBE faces considerable risk for seat-based revenues, but investors may be underestimating the collective defense from robust GAAP profitability, share repurchases, and potential for AI to pose benefits to compensate for the risks. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

ADBE Stock Price

I last covered ADBE in February where I rated the stock a strong buy given my view that the bearish outlook and sentiment for software stocks was more than offset by the deeply discounted valuation. The stock has fallen 13% since.

The visible catalyst might feel indirect, but I expect incumbent resilience and financial strength to prevail in the end.

ADBE Stock Key Metrics

ADBE is a software company with deep integrations across creative and marketing applications. The company has integrated AI across its product suite including with its popular Firefly product. That however has not been enough to shake the AI boogeyman fears as investors appear concerned that adding AI to existing legacy products might not be enough to compete against newer competitors that are built from AI from the very beginning.

Nonetheless, the company has seen rapid adoption of its AI products, with Firefly seeing 45% QoQ consumption growth and Firefly Enterprise seeing customers growing 50% YoY.

While the stock appears to be pricing in pressures to the long term terminal value, the company is still growing. Revenues jumped 12% YoY to $6.4 billion, exceeding guidance of between $6.25 billion to $6.3 billion in revenue. The company generated $6.06 in non-GAAP earnings per share, beating guidance of $5.90 per share.

Management also guided the company to see solid results moving forward, with the second quarter expected to see up to $6.48 billion in revenue, representing 10.3% YoY growth. The market worries that this is a melting ice cube, but melting ice cubes don’t typically grow their top-lines at a double-digit clip.

Management expects the company to generate around $26.1 billion in revenue for the full year, representing 9.7% YoY growth. CEO Shantanu Narayen notably is set to step down from the CEO role after 18 years at the helm. I, as I suspect many others, interpreted this somewhat negatively as this is certainly not the most ideal circumstances to leave the post. Even so, sentiment has dominated the trading action and I expect actual numbers to still be the most important drivers over the long term.

On the conference call, management appeared confident in their business, noting that investors should “be assured that we're spending on the newer initiatives.” That said, they did note some challenges, such as that their “traditional stock business saw a steeper decline than we expected,” but their “focus remains on giving customers meaningful choice between stock and generative AI.” I expect investors to be looking for any signs of demand deterioration, even if the current numbers suggest anything but. Management noted that the headwinds in the stock business pressured growth rates by roughly 30 bps. Management noted that they “don't see any reason why the trends we've seen in the business historically would inflect to drive a different dynamic” but of course investors have clearly voted with their dollars that this time is different.

Is ADBE Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ADBE had seen its valuation compress due to AI fears and absolutely flatten out following the release of Claude Mythos. These feel more significant in nature than OpenAI discontinuing their video generation app Sora.

At recent prices, ADBE traded at just around 3.5x sales.

That has put the stock at a high-single-digit multiple of earnings.

I have faced a rude awakening with regards to AI disruption risks and squarely view estimates as being too optimistic. Given ADBE’s high exposure to seat-based pricing as well as AI’s likely “good enough” offerings for the lower-end yet important segment, I find it more likely that top-line growth slows to the mid-single-digit range as soon as next year, if not lower. Veteran investors may be wary of the risks of investing in stocks amidst a likely future revision lower in estimates yet here I am sticking with a “strong buy” rating. I am not going to attempt to defend any view that ADBE might see growth re-accelerate from here, I no longer see that as likely even as ADBE integrates more AI product offerings. But I expect revenue to be resilient as I expect larger and existing customers to face significant inertia in switching away to competing products. That doesn’t mean the company is completely out of the woods. The real risk may be pressure to the seat based pricing model as AI allows its customers to replace human labor with AI agents. It is extremely difficult to predict to what extent and how quickly such pressures might materialize, but the key point is that ADBE would be able to benefit from such cost reductions itself - after all gross margins remain very high with fixed costs representing the vast majority of the cost structure. I expect ADBE to be able to offset pressures from AI with this resiliency and AI-driven margin expansion. In contrast with my prior coverage, I no longer see as much multiple expansion upside as the growth engine is no longer as reliable as before. That said, the stock is still too cheap here and appears to offer a solid risk-reward profile given the reasonably leveraged balance sheet, robust profitability, and resilient business (albeit disappointment in growth). I can see the stock delivering around 15% annual returns moving forward, driven by the earnings yield, modest earnings growth, and minimal implied multiple expansion.

ADBE Stock Risks

It is possible that ADBE is outright disrupted by AI. Perhaps it is unable to hold on to legacy business as expected. This may lead to declines in both revenues and profits. Perhaps the company is unable to offset AI pressures with AI-driven efficiencies of its own. Perhaps competitors are able to take market share in dramatic fashion, helped by AI. Management might conduct expensive M&A in attempts to reignite growth, at the cost of shareholder dilution or pressures to the balance sheet.

ADBE Stock Conclusion

ADBE is still posting strong numbers and I expect it to do so at least in the near term. I now expect significant volatility within a matter of quarters as AI is likely to have a disruptive impact for the lower-end market. I however expect ADBE to benefit from the very risks it faces. ADBE is a core holding in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my string buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/13/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Macy's: Healthy 4% Dividend Amid Bump In Comp Sales
data.description:
With the stock market choppy and short term interest rates continuing to fade throughout 2026, many investors have increasingly turned to dividend stocks to boost their income portfolios this year. And yet many dividend trades, such as energy stocks that have benefited from soaring oil prices, have become crowded and expensive.

Macy’s (M), in my view, is an excellent combination of yield, value, and an operational turnaround. While it’s true that the company faces headwinds from a tough macroeconomy, there are green shoots in this classic retailer as it achieves positive comp sales in a tough retail landscape. With shares of Macy’s down ~15% since the start of the year, we have a compelling opportunity to buy this stock on the dip.

I last wrote a buy article on Macy’s in January, when the stock was trading at $21 per share. Since then, Macy’s has fallen ~10%, which to me only accentuates the value appeal of this stock. Holiday sales emerged stronger than expected, and I believe Macy’s current outlook for the current year to be quite conservative given recent trends. I’m reiterating my buy rating here.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Macy’s, here are what I consider to be the top reasons to be bullish on the stock in the long haul:

  • Driving improvement in comp sales via a business shift toward higher income consumers. The “Bold New Chapter” initiative is focusing on shuttering underperforming stores and upgrading existing ones with better lighting, more curated merchandising, and higher staffing levels. These initiatives are aimed at transforming Macy’s into a higher-end retailer with a more premium in-store shopping experience. So far, positive sales comps suggest this strategy is working.
  • Focused on efficiency rather than growth at all costs. Macy’s ended FY25 with 665 stores, shuttering 15 net locations in the year. Its focus on shrinking its store portfolio to the best locations is allowing the company to preserve its profitability.
  • Managing tariffs well. Gross margins are declining by less than 1 point y/y, with stronger merchandising and product margin helping to offset tariff burdens. There’s opportunity if tariffs end up getting reversed.
  • Healthy payout ratios. The company spent less than one-quarter of its FY25 FCF on dividends.

Stay long here: in my view, Macy’s remains an appealing value play.

Q4 recap

Let’s now go through Macy’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Macy’s revenue declined -1.7% y/y to $7.64 billion, which handily beat Wall Street’s expectations y $7.51 billion (-3.3% y/y) by nearly a two point margin.

We note that the revenue declines are principally a function of the company’s intentional, targeted store closures in order to remove underperforming locations and elevate the Macy’s brand. Amid a tough holiday quarter, we note that each of Macy’s core “nameplates” achieved positive comp sales, which excludes the headwind from store closures.

Macy’s achieved 0.4% comp sales growth (versus 2% in Q3; on a go-forward only including sustaining stores, comps were slightly stronger at 0.6%), while Bloomingdale’s was the star performer at 9.9% comps growth (accelerating versus 9% in Q3). Bluemercury, the company’s skincare and fragrances arm, achieved 1.6% comps growth, also accelerating 50bps versus 1.1% growth in Q3.

Management continues to highlight the success at bringing customers back to Macy’s reimagined stores through new brand launches and more curated in-store experiences. Per CEO Antony Spring’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Macy's nameplate achieved 0.6% go-forward comparable sales growth, representing a 190 basis point improvement versus last year and a 690 basis point improvement on a 2-year basis. Performance was led by the Reimagine 125 locations in digital. The Reimagine 125 comparable sales grew 1%. Stores that have received initiatives delivered positive comps in 7 of the past 8 quarters and serve as a strong proof point of our ability to return to growth. Performance reflects our customer-led focus, including clear and purposeful changes at Macy's. We've improved our assortment relevancy.

We're investing in staffing and events, and we're leveraging local market strengths. These initiatives are having a halo effect on Macy's omnichannel business and contributing to positive digital comparable sales results. Speaking of digital, Macy's digital channel represents approximately 1/3 of our annual sales. It is benefiting from a modernized Macy's- Official Site inspired by what we're doing in stores. Over the past year, we have shifted to a more editorialized approach that reinforces our fashion authority and facilitate shopping across categories that drives commerce”.

On the profitability side, Macy’s gross margins declined only -50bps y/y to 35.2%, reflecting a 60bps headwind from tariffs (without which the company actually would have seen gross margin expansion). Meanwhile, core adjusted EBITDA held steady even amid a slight boost to sales and marketing spending, declining -3% y/y to $837 million at a 10.6% margin (-20bps y/y). Pro forma EPS of $1.67 beat Wall Street consensus of $1.55 with 8% upside.

Healthy dividend coverage and cheap valuation against FY26 outlook

Let’s now shift into the crux of my buy thesis on Macy’s: the stock trades cheaply relative to its improving fundamentals, while investors also have a relatively safe dividend to bank on.

At current share prices near $19, Macy’s trades at a market cap of $4.97 billion. Netting off the $1.25 billion of cash and $2.43 billion of debt on Macy’s balance sheet (which as a reminder is quite long-dated, with the majority of maturities happening post-2032), the company’s resulting enterprise value is $6.15 billion.

For FY26, Macy’s has guided to $21.40-$21.65 billion in revenue, which reflects -2% to -1% overall decline, on a ~flat range for comparable sales (excluding the impact of store closures and net new openings). Meanwhile, the company expects 7.7-7.9% adjusted EBITDA margins against this revenue profile, indicating $1.68 billion in adjusted EBITDA at the midpoint.

This positions Macy’s valuation multiples at:

  • 3.7x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA
  • 9.5x FY26 P/E

We note that Macy’s multiples sit well below many retail peers:

Meanwhile, the stock’s recently boosted dividend offers extra incentive to be patient for a potential upward re-rating in multiples. The company recently boosted its quarterly dividend by 5% to 19.15 cents per share, or $0.766 per year (a 4.0% yield at today’s share prices).

The company doesn’t guide to cash flow, but in FY25, Macy’s generated $1.43 billion in operating cash flow and $1.84 billion in adjusted EBITDA, or 78% conversion from EBITDA to OCF. Assuming similar conversion in FY26, guidance midpoint EBITDA of $1.68 billion would yield $1.31 billion OCF in FY26. Taking off $800 million in planned capex leaves $531 million in free cash flow.

The company’s $0.766 annual dividend represents a $201 million annual dividend expense, using 263.0 million shares outstanding as of the end of Q4. This reflects a relatively modest and sustainable 38% payout ratio.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are risks that we need to keep in mind here. Right now Macy’s is focused on efficiency and closing underperforming stores, so we can give it a pass on current revenue declines - but eventually the company will need to showcase a path to growth. Also keep in mind that many retailers have called out a more promotional retail environment. Macy’s could be forced to keep up with discounts and sales in order to maintain market share, which may deepen its margin losses this year.

Regardless, I see more positives than negatives in this stock especially at single digit earnings multiples. Buy for the yield and stay patient through a re-rating

data.title: EVT: Market Pullback And Discount Present Another Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT) provides investors with a diversified portfolio of equity investments in a closed-end fund wrapper. The tilt is primarily toward more value-oriented investments, with financials, healthcare, and industrials often making up the largest sector allocations of the underlying portfolio. With the latest market volatility, we've been seeing added pressure on this fund from its leveraged approach. However, the fund going to an even wider discount from our prior update is also adding further pressure.

EVT Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.25
  • Discount/Premium: -9.17%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.31%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.11%
  • Leverage: 18.1%
  • Managed Assets: $2.399 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

EVT's investment objective is to "provide a high level of after-tax total return consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation."

They highlight that the fund will invest primarily in "dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and seeks to distribute a high level of dividend income that qualifies for favorable federal income tax treatment."

Thus, the "tax-advantaged" portion of its name, and as for the 2025 distribution classification, this has been achieved. The entire distribution was either qualified dividends or long-term capital gains. Both are relatively tax-friendly when compared to ordinary income rates for most investors.

The fund is leveraged, and that is always worth considering, as it will increase overall volatility and risk for investors. It also comes with added leverage costs, pushing the fund's total expense ratio up to 2.27%.

While the Fed was easing their benchmark rate, we are now in a period where inflation is expected to remain sticky or rise on the back of increased oil prices. Therefore, most investors are anticipating no cuts for this year—and even murmurs of possibly having to increase once again could be on the table.

Performance - Discount Remains Appealing

While the fund has not escaped the latest equity pressures brought on by the conflict in Iran, the fund has still provided some positive total returns since our prior write-up. During this time, the S&P 500 Index has been about flat, though that is the result of this latest equity downturn.

I continue to view EVT as an attractive fund to consider given its discount. In fact, the discount has moved slightly wider during this period as well. That would have pressured the fund's total returns even further relative to the actual underlying portfolio's results. Given that the discount/premium can add additional volatility, which can be seen as a risk, but for other investors, it can present opportunities.

At this current time, the fund's discount is quite wide relative to its longer-term average level of the last decade. On the other hand, this wider discount has been fairly persistent now for the last several years. This can be a sign that this is the new normal level—which wouldn't make it look like an overly large bargain currently.

That said, my argument would be that an even wider discount is relatively rare aside from severe market downturns. Thus, it is still presented as an attractive time to consider adding to one's position.

With the fund being a more value-oriented tilt, they benchmark against the Russell 1000 Value Index. They also include the ICE BofA Fixed Rate Preferred Securities Index. Then they also mix those two indexes together for a blended index benchmark when they provide performance comparisons. Historically, the fund has provided competitive results against the Russell 1000 Value Index directly, and then, when blended, has outperformed.

This is what we should expect to see during good times, with the equity exposure helping to boost the overall results relative to the preferred sleeve. Additionally, the fund is leveraged, so during periods of strong results, that is going to get boosted even further.

Distribution - Monthly Pay

The distribution rate for EVT comes in at 8.31%, and on a NAV basis, that rate works out to 7.54%.

Given the cost of leverage for EVT isn't expected to decline at the moment, net investment income isn't likely to see that added boost of easing interest rates. At the same time, the bulk of the distribution will be covered via capital gains in the underlying portfolio anyway. That has almost always been the case for this fund, and the numbers reflect that of the last five years. So while NII can be the more predictable portion of distribution coverage, it isn't the main component.

Of course, that also then brings up the idea that if we remain in an extended or deepening downturn for the equity market, that will pressure them to potentially have to cut. As we saw in the distribution chart above, that does occur from time to time, needing to make an adjustment. Fortunately, most of the adjustments have been increases. That said, during periods such as the Global Financial Crisis or the bear market of 2022, they had ended up reducing the payout.

EVT's Portfolio

The broader breakdown of the fund's portfolio shows ~80% in equities and the remainder in fixed-income/preferred offerings. The fund is also positioned overwhelmingly in U.S. holdings, accounting for ~93% of the fund. This is consistent with what we've seen regularly with this fund—so no big surprises on that front.

Additionally, the fund has regularly provided overweight allocation toward financials. That's made it one of the diversifier funds for investors that may be otherwise feeling like they are exposed to too much technology weightings. This can help bring them some balance in a diversified way, thanks to its 75 total holdings and heavy financial weighting.

In fact, if we are getting technical, the financial weighting has moved up nearly another point since our last update. Industrials now comes in second place ahead of healthcare, which previously had 12.31% and 14.01% weightings, respectively. Meaning we've seen a decrease in healthcare exposure by nearly 5 points, which was partially offset by technology climbing up from the ~6% weighting before.

In the grand scheme of things, these are some meaningful sector shifts, but over a 9-month period that we are looking at between holdings, this can come from natural market price shifts. Though given the fund's 45% portfolio turnover rate, they were relatively active during this last fiscal year. That could be compared with the average turnover rate they had of 34.8% in the last five years.

In terms of the top holdings, we have seen JPMorgan (JPM) dethroned from its top spot, with Micron Technology (MU) climbing by its percentage weighting.

MU was not a top holding when we last looked at this fund; however, it was a holding listed in the fund around the same time. Looking at the N-PORT filed in September 2025 for holdings as of July 31, 2025, MU was around a 1.5% allocation in the fund. This is one of those names where some market price 'shifting' resulted in changes for the portfolio.

Primarily, that would be in the technology sector, and this was not just any market price shift but a surge in the stock's price. Even with the stock falling into bear territory as it is over 24% down from its 52-week high, the stock is still up nearly 230% since the end of July. This would be thanks to the shortage of memory chips on the back of aggressive AI infrastructure buildout.

Analysts expect this to help boost profits massively for MU over the next handful of years, followed by a relative plunge. On the other hand, even heading into 2028/2029 earnings plunge from estimated 2027 highs, those are still earnings well ahead of what was delivered in fiscal 2025.

Of course, whether these results are achieved or not, that will be the next huge question, as analyst estimates are merely that—estimates. What it has done for EVT, though, is provide some significant unrealized appreciation. At least for now, that is the sort of capital gain source that they can use to fund the monthly payout to investors.

Conclusion

EVT remains a discounted closed-end fund that is worth paying attention to based on an attractive valuation. Given the broader equity downturn, which has presented another added layer of enticement for considering a position. The fund has a long history of paying out monthly distributions to investors, going back to its inception in 2003. While they have had to make some adjustments over time, the monthly payout has been pretty predictable. During good market times, expect the distribution to remain stable or rise. During market downturns, if the distribution rate starts pushing near 10%, then one can consider that a lower distribution might be in the cards.

data.title: Papa John's: A Slice Of Dividend Remains Attractive And Sustainable
data.description:
In recent years, we have seen how vulnerable many fast-food restaurants can be to macroeconomic volatility. And even an established pizza chain like Papa John's International, Inc. (PZZA) is not entirely insulated as inflation continues to squeeze purchasing power and pricing flexibility. The challenge persists until today. That is why it's not surprising that its value still weakened by 1.44% from my previous coverage. Even so, we still can see its resilience as its efforts to stabilize its operations remain effective. Fundamentals promise sustainability and longevity. Valuation and technicals adhere to it as buying opportunities emerge again, although upside potential is not that much.

Papa John's: Some Resilience Amid Mounting Headwinds

The quick-service restaurants are facing multiple challenges amid macroeconomic volatility and stiff competition. These aspects continue to squeeze their pricing flexibility, sales growth, and margins. Even a popular pizza chain like Papa John's International, Inc. is not immune to these headwinds as the impact affects its performance. Yet, its prudent restaurant continues to help improve operational efficiency to stabilize cost pressures. This was visible in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $498.2M, down by 6.1% YoY from $530.8M. This was its weakest YoY revenue increase/decrease in four quarters. It is also worth noting that the Q4 revenue in the past two years has decreased continuously. This may suggest that the impact of softer market dynamics has intensified. And this was not surprising due to its weakness in the US market. If we look at its performance based on geographical diversification, its domestic operations remained hammered.

On a lighter note, its franchised royalties and fees still increased slightly. Additionally, international operations remained its cornerstone as sales grew by 8.1% YoY, which partially offset the weakness in the other segments. It still appeared manageable as a whole because the global systemwide sales remained flat at $1.23B. This proved that its international expansion remained helpful.

Likewise, its operating costs and expenses decreased. I believe this was impressive even after the increase in its restaurant count amid the rising prices. This may be due to its prudent and well-balanced split between running company-owned restaurants and franchising. While it was not enough to offset the decrease in revenue, it helped PZZA maintain positive profits. In the end, its operating margin remained decent at 4.9% despite its decrease from 5.6% YoY.

Possible Risks To Investors

Mounting inflationary headwinds remain a problem as they continue to affect purchasing power and discretionary spending. Although it has slightly slowed down recently, it remains above the 2.0% Fed target. So, we may infer that inflation is still not under control yet.

Its lagging performance in its domestic and North American markets may remain a challenge for PZZA. Although the number of restaurants increased to 3,523 versus 3,514, systemwide sales in the region remained weak. This can imply two things. One is that customer foot traffic in PZZA store chains there continued to decrease. Second is that it continued to lower its pricing to attract volume. But whatever the reason is, investors can't deny the challenges it faces in the region.

Domino's Pizza (DPZ) is also a primary challenge for PZZA. It continues to dominate the fast-food-style pizza market with its vast global market presence. On top of that, its strong delivery arm and high investment in technologies continue to challenge its peers. Its sustained revenue growth shows that it continues to gain market share relative to peers like PZZA and even Pizza Hut (YUM).

Risk Mitigants

Despite these challenges, I believe that PZZA has some strengths to mitigate the risks. Although these aren't enough to drive growth, these aspects can help it protect its profitability and sustainability.

One aspect to consider is its business model. PZZA is geared more towards franchising than running owned restaurants. Its franchised restaurants are the main reason for its restaurant growth. And if you look back at its revenues, you can see that franchise royalties and fees still increased. This shows the resilience of this model. This should not be surprising because more franchised restaurants means more franchise revenues. The amount or percentage it gets from franchised restaurants is based on their sales and not net income. So, PZZA is sure to generate extra revenues. Additionally, this model is low-cost, unlike running owned restaurants because it does not have to worry about their day-to-day expenses. This allows it to expand without bleeding out or borrowing huge amount. That is why its operating cash inflow remains enough to cover capex and dividends. This is supported by its expansion in the international market. In fact, 99% of PZZA stores there are franchised with robust demand considering their systemwide sales growth there. My concern is that they are still not enough to completely offset the weakness in the North American segment.

Its liquidity may also act as its support to ensure longevity of its increasing capacity and dividends. Cash and equivalents are still stable at $37M, which is enough to cover the current portion of its borrowings. If I add accounts receivables, it can also cover accounts payable, lease liabilities, and deferred revenues. PZZA complements it with prudent debt management through timely issuances and repayments. With that, the maturity profile of borrowings remain long since 99% of the amount will not mature within a year. To be more specific, 95% has a maturity of about four years. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 3.4x, which means that it only needs 3.4 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. Hence, you can prove that it still has enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update its Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $1.84 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $0.46, which gives a yield of 5.16%. The dividend growth rate based on the three-year CAGR is 6.11%. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 5.5%. I realized that 5.0% is a bit low to be conservative. That way, we can price in more risks. Lastly, its Beta is 1.16, which means that the stock price of PZZA has become more volatile. This also shows that its price swings are wider than that of the S&P 500. With all this information, the TP or target price is $39.40, which suggests a 10% upside potential.

Technicals

PZZA's technicals are still weak today as the price remains close to its one-year low. The 50-day SMA is still in a downtrend, which means that recent market sentiments have weakened. On a lighter note, we can see recovery attempts. The recent price increase has broken above the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is stronger than the average in the past 50 days. Also, the most recent low of $31.51 did not break below the previous low of $30.43. This means that buyers are starting to enter again and fewer sellers are accepting any price below $30.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show some improvements. The MACD line is increasing above the signal line, which means that buying volume is increasing as well. The histogram is back to positive territory, which implies that buying volume has outpaced selling volume recently. Lastly, RSI shows that the buying trend was due to overselling. At this point, the trend has normalized, so the uptrend may slow down. Nonetheless, it is still below the overbought region, so there is still enough buying volume. The only challenge is that their trend remains consistent with the price, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence that may lead to a breakout.

Conclusion

Papa John's International, Inc. remains resilient with its increasing international expansion to find more opportunities in less mature markets and lower its exposure to competition with DPZ. Its fundamentals and well-diversified business model also support its sustainability and dividend longevity. Meanwhile, valuation is justifiable with some upside potential, although not that much to entice buying surge. Technicals are still weak, but buying opportunities are present. I'm reiterating my buy rating, although I still advise caution amid macroeconomic volatility.

data.title: GUSH: Immediate Uncertainty And Conflict Risks Dominate
data.description:
The Direxion Daily S&P Oil & Gas Exp & Prod Bl 2X ETF (GUSH) is an E&P leveraged ETF that effectively plays movements in prices of oil on a leveraged basis. Everything is about the Iran War right now, and the ceasefire talks that seem to have fallen through. In particular, the US now seems intent on blockading the Strait themselves to prevent Iran from benefiting from tolls and to prevent logistics for oil occurring with Iranian ports. This could go either way:

  • It means a higher risk of conflict escalation which is bad for oil supply.
  • It could be successful, meaning de-mining efforts succeed, transit can occur with friendlier Gulf states under the protection of the US, and the considerable tolls that Iran would sporadically levy and put a high floor on oil price would be prevented.

If the Strait remains highly risky for all traffic as before, we note that there is some but not a huge amount of supply that could come back online that was withdrawn from markets by non-Gulf OPEC+ members these last few years where demand has been week, but it would not be enough to cover the shortfall completely (around 33%) and new asset development would take a while.

The uncertainty is good for oil prices, and we do expect that aborted transits, significant insurance costs or risks associated with uninsurability all should help the performance of GUSH on the open. But chasing the trade is very risk given the uncertainty, where the incentive is very high to open up the Strait by the US and the friendlier Gulf states. If the blockade is successful at protecting trade with non-Iranian ports and transits start to recover, GUSH could perform poorly. It is very risky, but at least the quirks of leveraged ETFs and their daily resetting mechanism are less of a concern given how imminent the trading factor is.

A note on leveraged ETFs

Because they reset daily after mimicking changes in the index that day by a 2x factor in the case of GUSH, there is the problem of value erosion. While a 1% rebound after a 2% drop isn't so bad for the underlying index, having a 4% drop and a 2% rebound is more of a problem. There is a reason why Warren Buffett's #1 rule is, don't lose money. If you lose money, you have less to recover with, meaning for every drop you need a bigger percentage recovery to bring you back to square 1. If an asset drops 33%, you need an almost 50% recovery to recover. If an asset drops 50%, you need 100% recovery to breakeven. Even if the next day is a bigger rebound than what you lost the previous day, with leveraged ETFs it is still less helpful even if the recovery gets doubled because more money was lost the prior day.

If you don't fully understand these risks, do not proceed with a leveraged ETF. They are best used over short durations because of value erosion. They are highly speculative burst instruments.

Links for reference on these risks:

  • The Lowdown on Leveraged and Inverse Exchange-Traded Products
  • Leveraged and Inverse ETFs: Specialized Products with Extra Risks for Buy-and-Hold Investors
  • Regulatory Notice 09-31 | FINRA.org

GUSH Breakdown

Like all Direxion ETFs, they offer a pretty low cost way to get some leverage into the portfolio, especially useful for allocators that that don't have access to leverage through their brokers. We already explained the risks with leveraged ETFs, amounting to value erosion, which is the trade off for the access and for the lower costs compared to typical margin-based leverage. The gross expense ratio is 1.03%, which isn't that much of a premium, around 0.6%, to more narrow ETFs such as a few of the iShares E&P ETFs like the (IEO).

We reiterate that this is mostly an underlying E&P exposure and therefore highly indexed to oil prices. There are also some refinery exposures. Since capacity for crude and refined crude product are both affected by the logistics threats associated with the Strait of Hormuz (since the Gulf has both oil wells and refinery capacity that are all backed up by the conflict), and this is a story dictated by supply factors, they will be trading in a highly correlated way for the moment so not too much distinction needs to be made as due to the quirk of value erosion, Direxion ETFs are anyway mostly suitable for short burst trades and not for buying and holding anyway.

The uncertainty is very high now, as the announcement of the blockade was made recently. Oil futures markets still point to a normalisation by later in 2026, after September more or less, but the blockade could go either way as far as oil markets are concerned.

Bull Case

  • The blockade news significantly heightens conflict risks, making transit dangerous and therefore crimping supply to raise prices. Transits have already been aborted on the announcement of the breakdown of talks.
  • The stranded daily supply of crude and product from Gulf states can't find other routes easily or be easily replaced (maybe 33% of crude shortfall can be offset quickly), even when taking demand destruction into account, therefore there is insufficient supply relief from non-Gulf unused capacity to assuage oil markets.
  • Run cuts by Asian refineries who depend on gulf supply could sharply reduce product demand and raise prices for those that can hold onto share, like the integrated US E&P stocks that feature in the underlying benchmark for GUSH.
  • TACO trade may be possible on the blockade, and perhaps the situation reverts to what was before the weekend, with sporadic tolls and therefore a high floor on oil prices.
  • Generally, there is a considerable premium due to transit and general oil market risks on oil that persists and limits downside as everyone rushes to hoard and speculate on oil in a generally uncertain situation, with energy crises now becoming a more recurring thing.

Bear Case

  • The blockade could be successful, with some initial de-mining action already taking place by the US Navy, allowing safer transit with the deterrence of formal US military presence in the region from non-Iranian ports.
  • Perhaps the blockade also pressures the Iranians, who might be unable to hereafter collect sporadic tolls from vessels and whose ports will be blocked by the US Navy, into accepting the demands laid forth in Islamabad by the US delegation.
  • Some other end to the War might occur.
  • If the war doesn't resolve or the Strait opens, because the Gulf supply was really important for Asian refineries, there could be run-cuts due to insufficient logistics that could lighten global demand for crude.

Bottom Line

In the immediate term, we think that the heightened conflict risks and speculative demand from general uncertainty may win out and drive upward movement in GUSH, and that would be the appropriate sort of horizon to deploy leveraged ETFs in. But we don't exclude the possibility that the US Navy managed to foster a situation that permits significantly more oil transit than before. GUSH is risky, we wouldn't play it, but we'd lean more towards a buy than a sell - but we keep a hold.

data.title: Scotts Miracle-Gro: Sowing The Seeds Of Future Growth
data.description:
Shares of Scotts Miracle-Gro (SMG) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining over 20%. After years of struggles that left the company with a weak balance sheet, SMG has made material progress in reducing leverage, optimizing inventory, and exiting an ill-fated venture into the cannabis supply business. I last covered shares in November, rating SMG a “buy,” and shares have performed nicely since then, adding 11% while the market is down slightly. Shares reached my $68-70 price target and have since pulled back modestly. With updated financials and an ever-changing macro environment now is a good time to revisit Scotts Miracle-Gro.

Now, given the recent developments in the Middle East, there has been concern about a potential slowdown in consumer spending as higher gasoline prices crowd out discretionary activity. While the duration of the disruption is unknowable, I expect prices to remain above pre-war levels for quite some time, given the magnitude of the lost output, and I view the risk as being a more prolonged disruption rather than a quicker return to normal. The first question is how this will impact demand for SMG’s lawn-and-garden oriented products.

I would describe this segment as being “quasi-discretionary.” In other words, demand clearly has some linkage to economic activity, but spending is not nearly as discretionary as other categories, like travel. In particular, it depends on whether a recession is housing centric or not. If it is not, consumers often spend more time and more of their budget around their home. As you seen see below, spending in the category did not shrink during the 2001-2002 recession, and it actually surged during the COVID recession. However, in 2008-2010, spending plunged as foreclosures surged and the homeownership rate fell.

To the extent we see an economic downturn, housing is unlikely to be the cause in my view. Housing has not driven much of the economic growth over the past two years. Mortgage payments as a share of disposable income are still a bit below pre-COVID levels, even after years of high rates, and they are well below 2007 levels. To the extent there has been excesses, they sit elsewhere, in areas like private credit, not mortgage lending. Because of this, I would expect higher oil prices to pressure restaurant and travel spending much more than home and garden spending, a positive for Scott’s business.

Plus, whenever there is an inflation shock or an economic downturn, a significant concern in consumer-facing businesses is the extent to which consumers “trade down” to find savings and manage their budgets. SMG’s business is less exposed to this trend because its customers tends to be a bit higher income than the national average, as a disproportionate share are homeowners. Plus, fertilizer, mulch, and weedkiller are relatively inexpensive as a share of outdoor project spending. In fact, during the post-COVID inflation shock, there was no shift towards private labels with branded products actually taking market share. I view trade down risk as being very minimal in 2026.

As such, I do not see recent developments really altering the turnaround that is slowly but steadily graining traction. In the company’s fiscal first quarter, Scotts Miracle-Gro lost $0.77 as revenue declined 3% to $354 million. I would remind readers that SMG’s earnings are very seasonal since most lawncare activity is in Q2/Q3. Typically, the business loses money in Q1/Q4 and turns a significant profit in Q2/Q3.

As I have discussed in previous write-ups, SMG generates revenue when it sells product to retailers (like Lowe’s or Home Depot), not when the consumer buys the product from the retailer. As such, fluctuations in retail inventories can alter SMG revenue relative to end-demand. After being saddled with excess inventory in 2023, retailers are more closely managing their inventory and timing their purchases closer to expected consumer purchases. This activity drove the 5% year over year volume decline in Q1 as orders are being pushed into Q2. This behavioral shift by retailers should not impact full-year results, but it will likely even further exaggerate the seasonality of SMG’s business with less revenue in Q1 and even more in Q2.

We are seeing SMG’s efforts to improve efficiency and productivity pay off. Gros margins expanded 90bps to 25.4% while SG&A was down a sharper 7% to $106 million. As you can see below, SMG has been slowly taking price while benefiting from lower raw material costs. The war in Iran is likely to reverse some of this commodity deflation. In particular, I would note that Scotts is the number one brand in fertilizer, though this segment is less than a quarter of its business. The conflict has disrupted trade flows here. Given existing inventories, I do not see this as a significant issue for the 2026 spring gardening season, but it may challenge 2027 margins (depending on how much of the price increase can be passed on to consumers), depending on how long trade flows through the Strait of Hormuz are impaired.

SMG has also made continued progress reducing financial leverage. Debt/EBITDA is down to 4.0x from 4.5x a year earlier, and SMG is on track to end this year with leverage in the 3.7-3.8x area. Debt was down about $250 million over the past year to ~$2.5 billion. As a reminder, leverage had peaked above 6.5x as SMG worked through weak demand and its cannabis business, but a focus on normalizing inventories and rightsizing costs has returned its balance sheet back to a sustainable position. Shares offer a 4% dividend yield, and I view this payout as secure given its balance sheet improvement.

Speaking at a conference in March, management reaffirmed guidance for $275 million of free cash flow and mid single-digit EBITDA growth, which should help the business deleverage below 4x while paying the dividend. I expect consumer sales to grow about 2-4% this year as I do not expect the recent oil shock as being too deleterious to the business. That said, there clearly is some medium-term margin risk from higher input costs, though again I view this as a late 2026/2027 issue, given the timing of SMG’s revenue.

Back in November, I was targeting $275-300 million of free cash flow. I now see $270-$290 million, reflecting the fact that margins and consumer demand may be slightly weaker given pressure from oil. I continue to believe this business should trade at around a 7% free cash flow yield, reflecting long-term growth of ~3% and a better balance sheet. Indeed, we may see a return to share buybacks in about 12-18 months as leverage falls below 3.5x.

As such, I expect shares to return to $68 as we see Q2/Q3 come in-line with plan as retailers replenish inventory and consumer spending in the category shows little impact from higher energy cost. Accordingly, I reiterate SMG as a “buy” and would use the recent pullback as an opportunity to add shares as investors appreciate the relative resilience of the business to feared macro pressures.

data.title: TOPT: Ultra-Concentrated Basket Of Mega Caps That Is Unnecessarily Risky, A Hold
data.description:
Investors who are exploring options to get most out of the market recovery driven by de-escalation optimism have multiple ETFs to consider, starting with the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) as perhaps the simplest, intuitively evident, and time-tested option. However, they might want to go a bit further and experiment with a little more sophisticated products, like those offering concentration in exchange for the theoretical potential of higher returns. And the iShares Top 20 US Stocks ETF (TOPT) is one of the key names in this cohort. But does TOPT deserve a Buy rating? I do not think so.

My analysis confirmed that its ultra-concentrated portfolio encompassing the Magnificent Seven names and other U.S. heavyweights has solid growth, GARP, and quality characteristics. With that being said, I believe TOPT is only a Hold.

The problem is that the current market environment is notable for extreme volatility amid swings in the narrative. For example, as of writing this article, President Trump ordered "an immediate blockade of the Strait of Hormuz" as peace talks stalled, which, I suppose, looks quite bullish for oil and a little bearish for equities. And even though I still believe the consistent de-escalation is achievable, it is reasonable to stick with more diversified vehicles like IVV to avoid too deep drawdowns and enjoy stronger risk-adjusted returns. Besides, there is also something to dislike about TOPT's past performance (i.e., inability to beat the more diversified Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ)).

TOPT Strategy

As we know from the iShares website, the S&P 500 Top 20 Select Index is the basis of the ETF's strategy. And as we can already guess from its name, there is nothing particularly sophisticated about its methodology. As explained on page S-2 of the summary prospectus, it

... measures the performance of the 20 largest U.S. companies by float-adjusted market capitalization within the S&P 500® (the “Parent Index”) as determined by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC (the “Index Provider” or “SPDJI”).

Importantly, certain constraints are applied to constituents' weights:

At each quarterly rebalance in March, June, September, and December, the weight of any single issuer will not exceed 22.5% of the Underlying Index; and the aggregate weight of issuers with individual weights exceeding 4.5% will not exceed 48% of the Underlying Index.

TOPT Portfolio Analysis

So what is the product of this ultra-concentrated approach? As of April 7, there are 21 equities in the TOPT portfolio, which is surprisingly higher than the index's target constituent count, but it can be quickly explained by the fact that Alphabet Inc. is represented by Class A (GOOGL) and Class C (GOOG) shares.

The key ten companies in this portfolio account for 73.7% of its net assets, and, yes, TOPT is notably overweight in all of them compared to IVV.

Company Ticker Weight in TOPT, as of April 7 Weight in IVV, as of April 7 Sector Market Cap, $ Billion, as of April 10
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) 16.14% 7.62% Information Technology 4,327.12
Apple Inc. (AAPL) 13.88% 6.55% Information Technology 3,721.67
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) 6.63% 3.13% Communication 3,686.83
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) 5.93% 4.87% Information Technology 2,764.49
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) 5.30% 2.50% Communication 3,686.83
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) 4.68% 3.68% Consumer Discretionary 2,294.80
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) 4.55% 2.79% Information Technology 1,581.24
Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) 4.48% 1.54% Financials 1,031.03
JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) 4.10% 1.41% Financials 802.10
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) 4.09% 2.21% Communication 1,454.62
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) 3.96% 1.72% Consumer Discretionary 1,300.78

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

My calculations show that TOPT is overweight in all stocks it offers exposure to compared to the S&P 500 ETF, from NVDA (overweight by 8.5%) to AMZN (overweight by 1%). And in IVV, they account for 47% of the net assets.

Next, what is TOPT's factor mix and how is it different from that of IVV? I would say it looks undoubtedly robust, especially when it comes to growth, GARP, quality, and momentum characteristics. Value is its Achilles heel though, so let us start the more in-depth review with this factor.

Value: Multiples Are Expectedly Lofty

Since 69.6% of TOPT's net assets are allocated to the trillion-dollar league, its weighted average market cap is 1.86x that of IVV.

At the same time, its WA earnings yield is slightly lower, while P/S and EV/EBITDA ratios are higher. This is, obviously, in part the consequence of the fact that TOPT completely ignores four GICS sectors, namely industrials, real estate, materials, and utilities, and meaningfully overweights IT and communication.

Growth And GARP

In terms of growth, TOPT is considerably ahead of IVV, as illustrated by its WA forward revenue, earnings per share, and EBITDA growth rates and the allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Growth grade or higher.

And these impressive growth rates (mainly resulting from NVDA's contribution) also support decent GARP characteristics. More specifically, its PEG ratio (calculated as the weighted average TTM Price/Earnings ratio divided by the forward EPS growth rate), the PSG ratio, and the EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratio are notably lower than those of IVV.

Quality: Close To Exemplary

There is almost nothing to criticize on the quality front.

  • More specifically, the TOPT portfolio encompasses 20 bellwether U.S. companies, with 19 of them having an A+ Quant Profitability grade or higher.
  • There are no loss-making names in its portfolio, and its weighted average NI stands at impressive $76.5 billion, 1.8x larger than IVV's, as per my calculations.
  • Capital efficiency metrics are notably stronger than those of IVV.

Momentum And Low Volatility

In terms of momentum, there is also something to appreciate. For instance, TOPT has 70.8% of the net assets allocated to stocks with a B- Quant Momentum grade or higher vs. 61.4% of IVV. The flip side is high beta of its portfolio, which can, obviously, result in a much deeper maximum drawdown in a correction or a bear market. Nevertheless, high beta names tend to capture well over 100% of the market's upside during recoveries.

TOPT Performance

Despite overall solid results, performance-wise, TOPT has a few issues that I find worrisome.

First, even though ultra-concentration has secured its outperformance versus the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF and the iShares S&P 100 ETF (OEF) since its inception in October 2024, it has nonetheless trailed the tech-heavy Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF and the iShares Nasdaq Top 30 Stocks ETF (QTOP).

In other words, the TOPT investors took additional risks with a more concentrated portfolio but were not sufficiently rewarded for that.

And looking at the annualized data for the November 2024–March 2026 period, we see that TOPT has not only underperformed a much better diversified QQQ and QTOP in terms of annualized returns but also delivered weaker risk-adjusted returns measured using Sharpe and Sortino ratios, which is partly the consequence of its high standard deviation.

Final Thoughts

In the debate on concentration and diversification, I gravitate toward the golden mean. In other words, I am equally skeptical about ultra-concentrated portfolios and extra-diversified ones. And TOPT goes a little bit too far with its focus on just 20 bellwethers. That is to say, cutting 480 constituents from the S&P 500 looks like a seamless and elegant way to boost total returns, but in practice, it means enduring quite deep drawdowns and much higher volatility, potentially leading to much softer risk-adjusted returns compared to the S&P 500 ETF in the long run. And TOPT's 20 bps expense ratio (vs. IVV's 3 bps) is also a detractor not to overlook.

The truth is, TOPT has a robust factor mix with a GARP tilt and exceptional quality, but I would prefer to see a sub-1 PEG ratio supported by more adequate sector representation and not depending on just a handful of names.

To sum up, even though TOPT has a few advantages, I see no sufficient arguments why I should assign a Buy rating to it.

data.title: Southern First Bancshares: Progress Is Great, But Not Great Enough To Bank On
data.description:
In my search for new and interesting companies to dig into, I have finally come across a bank by the name of Southern First Bancshares (SFST). With a market capitalization of $448.6 million, it is a bit on the smaller end of the spectrum. But in recent years, management has been working hard on growing the company. They have actually had some remarkable success in this regard, with both the balance sheet and income statement improving. Asset quality has been getting better, and the company stands to benefit from continued expansion as time goes on. However, I do have some issues with it. Despite the improvements in asset quality, the readings reported by management are still below what I consider to be attractive. What's more, shares are expensive relative to earnings. And even though that would change as the business continues expanding, we aren't at a low enough price to justify anything other than a 'hold' right now.

I'm Not Ready to Bank on Southern First Bancshares

According to the management team at Southern First Bancshares, the company operates as the holding company for a South Carolina state bank by the name of Southern First Bank. From its origins back in 1999, the company has expanded to have 8 retail offices in South Carolina, 3 retail offices in North Carolina, and a single retail office in Atlanta, Georgia. It also has a loan production office in South Carolina and is working on opening another retail office in North Carolina by the end of this year.

Through the locations that the business has in operation, it has grown to service an important part of the states in which it operates. In the image above, you can see a breakdown of its deposits compared to the deposits of all banks in those markets combined. It actually has a rather sizable stake in Greenville, with the business responsible for 6.8% of total deposits. In other markets, the firm is less prevalent. However, these are spaces that are considered attractive in general.

For instance, in Greenville, the median household income was around $75,881 in 2024. That was below the $83,730 national average that we saw in the US that year. However, where the company might fall short in terms of the quality of the market, it does have the benefit of operating in an area that is quite heavily populated. The market has a rather robust population of 1.02 million. The Charleston and North Charleston metropolitan statistical area that the company has three offices in has a population of 893,430. And unlike Greenville, it actually has a higher-than-average median household income totaling $90,307. In the chart above, you can see data regarding the other markets in which the business operates. Although the median household income is low in some of these markets, the company makes up for that with the fact that the markets in which it operates have significant population sizes. And in Atlanta, where the company has a single office, median household income is high at $92,344, while the total population is an astounding 6.50 million.

Over the last few years now, management has seen some pretty good growth when it comes to the balance sheet of the business. In the chart below, you can see that deposits have expanded from $3.38 billion to $3.72 billion. About $552.9 million, or 14.9% of the total, were high-cost brokered deposits borrowed from other financial institutions. But that's not unexpected for such a small bank. Many smaller financial institutions use brokered deposits in order to grow, even if it means lower profitability. The only thing that I don't like about this is that their reliance on them has increased in recent years. Back in 2023, for instance, only $379.4 million, or 11.2%, of its deposits were classified as brokered. The bigger concern that I have is that the firm ended 2025 with about $1.5 billion of deposits classified as uninsured. That happens to be 40.4% of all deposits. That is certainly higher than the 30% maximum I typically like to see. So that is a knock against the enterprise.

As deposits have grown, the value of loans on its books has increased also. That number grew from $3.56 billion to $3.80 billion over the last three years. The good news is that these loans seem to be pretty diversified. Yes, we have a large amount of consumer real estate, totaling about 30% of all loans. We also have 24.9% tied up in non-owner-occupied commercial real estate. Considering my own worries about high and rising vacancy rates, this is a bit concerning. Nationally, for instance, the office vacancy rate right now is 17.6%. But in Atlanta, it is 17.9%. The good news is that this is an improvement over the 19.2% reported a year earlier. But over that same window of time, the vacancy rate in Charlotte has increased from 15.5% to 17.8%.

Other parts of the balance sheet have changed also. Perhaps the most noteworthy, however, is that even as cash and cash equivalents expanded from $156.2 million to $269.6 million, debt has dipped from $311.3 million to $264.9 million. That is great to see in and of itself. It certainly deserves some attention. And as the composition of the company's balance sheet has changed, and as the size of it has grown, overall revenue and profitability have expanded.

In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean here. Net interest income, non-interest income, and net profits have all increased over the last three years. This is not only because of the change in the size of the company's balance sheet. We also need to factor in that we have seen an improvement in net interest margin. It jumped from 2.06% in 2024 to 2.57% in 2025. The chart below shows this. But it also shows the cost or yield of certain assets and liabilities and how they have changed over the same window of time. Crucially, even as loans have become more profitable for the business, deposits have become cheaper since 2024. This makes sense considering that the Federal Reserve has been slashing interest rates and that interest-bearing deposits typically happen to be highly variable in nature. That means that management can cut how much it is paying depositors as the Federal Reserve cuts rates.

One thing that certainly helps the company here is that, as opposed to many other banks, most of its loans are fixed in nature. At the end of 2025, 74.5% of all loans were fixed, while the remainder were floating. This gives the company some wiggle room even in a declining interest rate environment. It doesn't mean that profitability won't eventually contract. It could, but it means that the decline, if it occurs, will be lagged.

If we use the data provided by management for the 2025 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced relative to earnings. This is one big issue that I have with the institution. I typically like to see that the price-to-earnings multiple is no higher than 10. But at 14.8, the company is well above that mark and is above four of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. It is positioned better when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples, as the chart below illustrates. It is on the cheaper end of the spectrum in this regard. But that alone is not enough to justify a bullish outlook.

We are starting to see asset quality for the company improve. In the first chart below, you can see how return on assets has increased over the last three years. But even with this improvement, the company is worse off than any of the five institutions that I compared it to. Unfortunately, the return on assets reading that we get here is below the 1% minimum that I like to see. The subsequent chart below shows a similar scenario where only one of the five companies has a lower return on equity than Southern First Bancshares does. What's more, the 8.73% reading that we have here is below the 10% minimum that I look for.

Lastly, we do have one positive note to leave off on. And this relates to non-performing assets and loans. As the chart below illustrates, these have worsened over the last three years. But considering that I like to see that no more than 0.50% of both loans and assets are non-performing, and that the company is still well below these levels, I must say that I am impressed with this as a whole.

Takeaway

When you take all the data that's available and add it together, you see a business that is definitely showing signs of improvement. At some point in the future, it might become a rather attractive play. But between high uninsured deposits, high brokered deposits, how expensive the stock is in relation to earnings, and subpar asset quality, the best rating that I can assign it right now is a 'hold.'

data.title: IXN: Global Tech Leadership Remains, Eyeing A New Record High
data.description:
Global technology stocks petered out last October. Their high was well before equities more broadly reached their peak in January and February. Indeed, the iShares Global Tech ETF (IXN) remains below last year's zenith, but significant headway has been made just in the past two weeks.

Coming off the All-Country World Index’s best two-week rally going back to November 2020, the thrust is being led by some of the downturn's weakest areas, like IXN. I had a buy rating on the ETF back in July of 2025. Shares are up 17% since then, and I reiterate a buy rating today. I’ll provide a refreshed look at the fundamentals, valuation, seasonality, and technical situation.

IXN: Alpha to the World of Stocks YoY, Sharp Comeback Lately

According to the issuer, IXN offers investors exposure to electronics, computer software and hardware, and information technology companies and seeks to provide access to technology stocks from around the world. It’s primarily an Information Technology sector ETF, but it also holds holdings from other geographies outside the US, which helps from a diversification perspective.

IXN is a growing ETF, now with $6.8 billion in assets under management as of April 10, 2026. That’s up from just $4.3 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 39 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is modest at only 1.01%--significantly below that of the ACWI.

Share-price momentum has been strong lately, despite the aforementioned stagnant price action back to the early part of the fourth quarter of last year. With an A ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, I’ll note key levels on the chart to monitor later in the article. As it stands, the product can be risky at times, evidenced by elevated volatility. The current implied volatility has retreated to 36%, implying a daily swing of more than 2%.

Finally, in terms of liquidity, IXN sports decent metrics. Average daily volume is near 300,000 shares, while its median 30-day bid/ask spread is somewhat tight at 8 basis points.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar plots along the upper row of the style box, indicating its bent to global large caps. Importantly, from a diversification point of view, there’s 25% allocated to international equities, which is a good thing when ex-US beats domestic shares. More than half of the fund is invested in large-cap growth, so the secular story of AI penetration is a key driver of IXN’s alpha.

Right now, the price-to-earnings ratio is very reasonable at under 18x. Recall last July, it was above 27x. Paired with a long-term earnings growth rate of 19.1%, the resulting PEG ratio is below 1, which is a solid GARP bargain, in my view.

IXN: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

The 100% Information Technology ETF is highly concentrated, adding to its risk profile. Between NVIDIA (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL), that’s more than a quarter of the portfolio right there. Microsoft (MSFT), the only Mag 7 stock to be negative over the past 12 months, is also a hefty position at more than 9%.

Beyond that, diversification is decent. In all, the top 10 stocks account for an elevated 57% of the fund. Be on guard for potential volatility later this month when mega-cap tech begins reporting Q1 results.

IXN: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal perspective, we are right ahead of what has been a very bullish period. The May through August stretch has been IXN’s best when scanning price return data over the past 10 years.

Add this to the bullish-indicator camp.

IXN: Bullish Calendar Stretch Ahead

The Technical Take

With a compelling valuation, alpha over the past year, and strong seasonals, IXN’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares broke down below their long-term 200-day moving average but then sprouted right back above it. Now, the ETF is back to the breakdown point from earlier this year. I’d like to see another thrust toward the all-time high of $113 from last October on stronger momentum. The 60 RSI level is indeed not quite as high as I’d like.

Also take a look at where long-term support lies—just under $90. If we take the measured move upside price objective based on the mid-2024 to April 2025 decline and subsequent breakout, $114 is the target—the October 2025 high. So, a correction down to $95 was certainly reasonable. Recall that I calculated that $114 level last July, so everything has been playing out as the technicals would script.

Today, with a bullish false breakdown in play, I expect new highs later this year. The bulls will have to crack through a somewhat high amount of volume by price up to the $110 level. If so, it should be clear skies above.

IXN: Technical Target Hit, Pullback Appears Complete, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on IXN. The ETF has done all the right things, technically. The valuation, meanwhile, is downright compelling, with a PEG ratio under 1 heading into a bullish calendar period.
